segments.s333-g1: An epithet + name followed by a verbal phrase. جهاندیده (epithet) precedes the name گودرز as a modifier ('world‑wise/experienced Goudarz'). برپای خاست is a poetic past action: برپای (lit. 'on his feet', here 'to his feet') + خاست (archaic past of خاستن, 'rose/arose'), yielding 'rose to his feet.'
segments.s333-g2: بیاراست is here a verbal/adjectival perfective from آراستن meaning 'put in order / prepared / adorned (himself)' and links to the following clause. با + شاه marks the addressee ('to/with the king'). گفتار راست is a noun phrase in which گفتار 'speech' is qualified by راست 'straight/direct' — 'a direct/straight speech.' Word order is typical literary Persian: verb/adjectival verb phrase + prepositional phrase marking the audience + noun phrase object.
segments.s333-g3: چنین گفت is the fixed phrase 'thus said' (چنین 'thus' + گفت 'said'); کای is the vocative particle 'O' addressing the king (not the title Kay here). The sequence شاه پیروز بخت is a nominal phrase in apposition: 'king, victorious in fortune' (پیروز qualifying the following noun phrase بخت in a compound sense).
segments.s333-g4: ندیدیم is the simple past/ perfect meaning 'we have not seen' (negated past). چون here means 'like' (comparison) and introduces the comparison to تو 'you' (second‑person vocative/object). خداوند تخت is a two‑word title 'lord/sovereign of the throne' (خداوند used as 'sovereign/lord' here; تخت = 'throne').
segments.s333-g5: A range expression formed with ز ... تا meaning 'from ... to ...': ز گاه منوچهر تا کیقباد = 'from the (reign/time of) Manuchehr to Kay Qobad.' Note the literary order where گاه 'time/era' follows ز and precedes the ruler's name.
segments.s333-g6: The same z...ta pattern repeats: ز کاووس تا گاه فرخ نژاد = 'from Kavus to the time/era of Farrokh‑Nezhad.' گاه again marks 'time/era'; فرخ نژاد here functions as a personal name (two elements that individually mean 'fortunate' and 'lineage').
segments.s333-g7: به پیش بزرگان is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in the presence of / before the great ones' (به + پیش). کمر بسته‌ام is a perfect construction: کمر 'waist/girdle' + بسته‌ام (past participle بسته + enclitic ‑‌ام 'I have'), idiomatically 'I have girded my loins' = 'I have prepared myself.'
segments.s333-g8: This clause uses the negative perfect form ننشسته‌ام (ن + نشسته + ‑ام) 'I have not sat/ I have not remained [sitting]'. بی‌آزار precedes it and literally means 'harmless/without harm' but here with the negation it functions idiomatically: 'I have not sat even a single day harmlessly/idle' — i.e. 'I have not been idle a single day.' یک روز 'one day' is the time expression emphasized by the negative.
segments.s333-g9: نبیره پسر بود هفتاد و هشت is a nominal clause where نبیره پسر (a descendant/son) is followed by the copula بود in the past and then the number phrase هفتاد و هشت; in older/poetic word order the predicate-number follows the verb. Here بود is the past copula linking the subject phrase to the numerical complement.
segments.s333-g10: کنون ماند هشت و دگر برگذشت contains a temporal adverb (کنون, “now”) + past/formal perfective ماند “remained” with the numeral هشت as its complement; then و دگر برگذشت (“and the others passed away”) uses دگر as a pronominal ‘the others’ and برگذشت (poetic past of گذشت) meaning ‘passed on/passed away.’
segments.s333-g11: همان گیو بیداردل هفت سال places the demonstrative/emphatic همان (“that/the same”) before the proper name گیو and an epithet بیداردل; the duration هفت سال is given here but the copula linking it to the verb comes in the next segment (i.e. the duration attaches to the upcoming بود).
segments.s333-g12: به توران زمین بود is a locative clause with به + place (به توران زمین) and the past copula بود “was.” The following predicates بی‌خورد و هال describe his condition: بی‌خورد is a compound negative adjective ‘unfed/poverty‑stricken’ and هال is a nominal for ‘state/condition,’ linked by و.
segments.s333-g13: به دشت اندرون is a locative phrase (‘into/in the inner plain’). گور بد خوردنش is a nominal clause where گور ‘the grave’ is presented as the agent and بد modifies خوردنش (the nominalized خوردن), producing the idiomatic poetic sense ‘the grave ill‑ate him’ — i.e. he was grievously taken by the grave (nearly died).
segments.s333-g14: هم از چرم نخچیر پیراهنش is material‑of phraseing: از introduces the material (‘from/of’), چرم نخچیر = ‘the leather of prey/game,’ and پیراهنش is the noun plus the possessive clitic ش ‘his.’ هم ‘even/also’ emphasizes that even his shirt was made from prey’s hide.
segments.s333-g15: به ایران رسید is a verb phrase ‘reached Iran’ (به + place + رسید). آنچه بد شاه دید is a relative clause ‘that which the ill‑disposed king saw / what displeased the king’ serving as the subject/thing that arrived; note the relative pronoun آنچه prefaces the clause before the verb.
segments.s333-g16: که here introduces an explanatory/causal clause. تیمار او literally ‘his treatment/care’ (او = him) and گیو is the subject; چندی modifies کشید, a past poetic perfect of کشیدن used here in the sense ‘to endure/suffer’ — so the clause means ‘Giv endured that treatment for a while.’
segments.s333-g25: بفرمود is a literary/polite past of فرمودن (he said/ordered) placed before its object, a common poetic word order. عهد قم و اصفهان is the object phrase ('a covenant/treaty concerning Qom and Isfahan'), with the two place-names joined by و.
segments.s333-g26: نهاد here is the past verb (نهاد = placed/established) with its omitted subject understood from context (the ruler). بزرگان و جای مهان are coordinated objects/phrases ('the elders/leading men and the place/station of the guests'), with noun+modifier order (جای مهان = guest-quarters/guest-station).
segments.s333-g27: نویسد is the literary present/narrative form of 'to write' placed before its subject (دبیر), a permitted poetic word order. ز مشک و ز عنبر is an instrumental/ornamental phrase ('with musk and with amber/ambergris') describing how the letter was prepared; دبیر is the agent (the scribe).
segments.s333-g28: یکی نامه is an indefinite/numeral phrase ('one/a certain letter'). از پادشا marks origin/author ('from the king'), and بر حریر is an adverbial phrase ('on silk' i.e. written on silk). Word order is determiner/noun + source + material.
segments.s333-g29: یکی مهر زرین is a noun phrase 'one/a golden seal.' بر او is a prepositional phrase 'upon it' (او refers back to the letter), and برنهاد is the past verb 'placed upon' (subject omitted, as usual in epic narrative).
segments.s333-g30: بر آن نامه = 'on that letter' (prepositional phrase). شاه آفرین functions as a compound object meaning 'praise of the king' or the formula 'Shah‑afarin,' کرد is the past verb 'made/caused/inscribed,' and یاد here means 'as a remembrance/memorial.' The clause says he added the royal praise as a memorial on the letter.
segments.s333-g31: که introduces a subordinate optative clause here (common in poetic wishes) and, with the optative particle باد at the end, yields 'May God be pleased with Goudarz.' ز is the poetic preposition 'with/about' (ز گودرز = with Goudarz). باد is the optative 'may.'
segments.s333-g32: دل بدسگالانش = 'the heart(s) of his ill-wishers' (بدسگالان + possessive -ش referring back to him). پر دود is an adjectival phrase 'full of smoke' (پر = full (of)), and باد again is the optative 'may' forming a wish: 'may their hearts be full of smoke.' The order is inverted for poetic effect.
segments.s333-g33: به ایرانیان (prepositional phrase) = “to the Iranians”; گفت is the past tense verb “said” placed before the speaker (inversion common in poetic prose) so the clause reads “said…”. گیو دلیر is the speaker’s name + epithet (proper name + adjective) following the verb: “Giv the Brave.”
segments.s333-g34: مبادا که آید ز کردار سیر is a prohibitive wish: مبادا introduces a negative optative (‘may it not be / let it not happen’). که introduces the subordinate clause; آید is 3rd-person present/subjunctive (‘may come / become’). ز + کردار سیر = “from/with respect to deeds’ satiety” i.e. ‘become satisfied by one’s deeds’; سیر here means ‘satiation, being content’. The whole clause means “may it not happen that one becomes content/satiated with his deeds.”
segments.s333-g35: بدانید is the plural imperative of دانستن used as an instruction ‘know / be aware’. کو introduces an indirect question ‘where’. یادگار من است is a copular clause ‘is my memento’ (یادگار = memento, من = my/I, است = is); word order places the verb at the end as usual.
segments.s333-g36: به نزد شما is a locative/prepositional phrase meaning ‘in your presence / with you’. زینهار is a noun ‘a warning, admonition’ (here ‘my warning’). من است together form the predicate ‘is mine’ (possessive sense); overall: ‘My warning is with you.’
segments.s333-g37: مر is the archaic/poetic form corresponding to مرا (‘to me’ / ‘as for me’) used here to mark the speaker’s stance. او را is the topicalized direct object (‘him’, with را marking definiteness/emphasis). همه پاک is an adverbial/adjectival phrase ‘entirely/purely’. فرمان برید functions as a verbal phrase ‘execute/carry out the command’ (فرمان = command; برید here in classical usage conveys performing/taking the command). Together: ‘As for me, treat him as wholly pure and carry out his command.’
segments.s333-g38: ز گفتار گودرز = ‘from / concerning Goudarz’s speech’ (ز = from/about; گفتار = speech; گودرز = proper name). بر مگذرید is a negative hortative: مگذرید here (in negative contexts) = ‘do not let pass / do not ignore’; the preposition بر intensifies the sense ‘over/aside’. So the clause means ‘do not let Goudarz’s words pass / do not disregard Goudarz’s speech.’
segments.s333-g39: ز گودرزیان = ‘from/among the Gudarzis’ (ز = from/among; گودرزیان = the people/followers/house of Goudarz). هرکه = ‘whoever’. بد پیش‌رو = ‘bad (in) the vanguard/front‑runner’ (بد = bad; پیش‑رو = one who goes before / leader/forerunner). The phrase picks out any Gudarzi who proves a deficient leader at the front.
segments.s333-g40: یکی is an indefinite ‘one / someone’ introducing a replacement. آفرینی here is a poetic noun meaning a (praiseworthy) creation / champion (contextually ‘a new hero’). بگسترد is an optative/3rd‑person form ‘may he spread / may he send forth’ (classical subjunctive/optative sense). نو is a postpositive adjective ‘new’ modifying the foregoing. Altogether: ‘may a new champion be put forth.’
segments.s333-g17: جهاندار (literally ‘world‑holder’) is a title = ‘the sovereign/king’. سیر آمد is a verbal phrase meaning ‘came/was come sated’ or ‘grew content’; سیر here = ‘sated’ (idiomatic: satisfied with something). از تاج و گاه literal ‘from crown and court/seat’ — a compact noun phrase giving the things he is sated with. Word order is topic (title) + verbal phrase + prepositional/nominal phrase describing what he is sated with.
segments.s333-g18: هم او چشم دارد به نیکی ز شاه = ‘he too has an eye toward the king’s goodness/favour’. هم = ‘also/too’; او = ‘he’; چشم دارد (literally ‘has an eye’) is idiomatic for ‘keeps watch/looks for/aims at’ followed by به + noun (purpose/aim). ز is the poetic form of از meaning ‘from’ (so ‘from the king’ = ‘the king’s’). The clause therefore expresses hope/expectation of favour rather than literal sight.
segments.s333-g19: چنین داد پاسخ = ‘thus gave an answer’ (چنین = ‘thus’; داد = past tense ‘gave’; پاسخ = ‘an answer’). The conjunction که introduces the content of the reply. بیش است از این literally ‘(it) is more than this’ — a predicative verbless clause with است as the copula.
segments.s333-g20: که بر گیو بادا هزار آفرین = a blessing/wish following the reply. که = ‘that’; بر گیو = ‘upon Giv’ (بر + proper name); بادا is the classical optative particle ‘may it be’ (a fixed, archaic wish form); هزار آفرین = ‘a thousand praises’ (numeral + noun). Word order places the optative after the indirect object, a poetic inversion (‘may a thousand praises be upon Giv’).
segments.s333-g21: خداوند گیتی ورا یار باد = ‘May the lord of the world be his ally.’ خداوند گیتی is a title ‘lord/master of the world’ (can mean God or a sovereign); ورا = archaic compound pronoun meaning ‘to him’ (ورا = به + او); یار باد is optative ‘may (he) be a friend/ally’. The optative باد follows the predicate as is common in poetic formulas.
segments.s333-g22: دل بدسگالانش پرخار باد = ‘May the hearts of his ill‑omened/ill‑wishing people be full of thorns.’ دل = ‘heart(s)’; بدسگالانش = بدسگالان + -ش (the plural ‘ill‑omened/ill‑wishing ones’ with third‑person possessive suffix ‘his/their’); پرخار = adjective ‘full of thorns/very thorny’ (پر‑ = ‘full of’ + خار ‘thorn’); باد is again the optative ‘may (it be)’. The construction is a imprecatory wish against his enemies.
segments.s333-g23: کم و بیش = idiom ‘more or less’ or ‘in any case’; ما پاک = ‘we (are) pure/innocent’ or ‘our affairs are clean/pure’ (پاک modifying ما or our state); بر دست = ‘in the hand/of the power of’ (بر + دست used idiomatically = ‘under the control of’); تست here is the archaic 2nd‑person copular form (classical توست) meaning ‘is yours’ / ‘is in your power’ — the surface lemma given corresponds to a different modern verb, so treat it as the poetic copula ‘توست’. The whole phrase says our fate/affairs lie in your power.
segments.s333-g24: که روشن‌روان بادی و تن درست = a following wish/clause introduced by که (‘that’): روشن‌روان = ‘bright‑souled, clear‑minded’ (an epithet/wish for disposition); بادی here is an optative form ‘may you be’ (poetic; related to باد ‘may’), so روشن‌روان بادی = ‘may you be bright‑souled’; و تن درست = ‘and (may) your body be sound/proper’ (تن = body; درست = ‘sound/right’). Note: some lemma senses given (e.g. بادی) list only literal meanings like ‘windy’ but here the form is the classical optative.

sentences.s333-s1: The world-wise Goudarz rose to his feet and, having composed himself, addressed the king with direct words.
sentences.s333-s2: Thus he said: 'O victorious king, we have not seen a sovereign of the throne like you.'
sentences.s333-s3: From the time of Manuchehr to Kay Qobad; from Kavus to the time of Farrokh‑Nezhad.
sentences.s333-s4: Before the great ones I have girded my waist; I have not sat (i.e. remained idle) a single day.
sentences.s333-s5: He had seventy‑eight sons (descendants); now eight remained and the others had passed away.
sentences.s333-s6: That Giv, wakeful‑hearted, was in the land of Turan for seven years, unfed and in a wretched state.
sentences.s333-s7: In the inner plain the grave ill‑took him, and even his shirt was of game‑hide.
sentences.s333-s8: The thing that displeased the king reached Iran; Giv endured its treatment for a while.
sentences.s333-s13: He commanded a covenant for Qom and Isfahan and set up the elders and the places for guests.
sentences.s333-s14: The scribe wrote, perfumed with musk and amber, a letter from the king on silk.
sentences.s333-s15: He placed a golden seal upon it; on that letter he inscribed the king's praise as a memorial.
sentences.s333-s16: May God be pleased with Goudarz; may the hearts of his ill-wishers be filled with smoke.
sentences.s333-s17: Giv the Brave said to the Iranians, “Let it not be that anyone becomes satisfied with his deeds.”
sentences.s333-s18: Know where my memento is; my warning is with you.
sentences.s333-s19: Consider him wholly pure and carry out his command; do not let Goudarz’s words go unheeded.
sentences.s333-s20: Among the Gudarzis, whoever proves a poor front‑runner, let a new champion be sent forth.
sentences.s333-s9: The world‑ruler, sated with crown and court, and at times he keeps his eye on the king’s favour.
sentences.s333-s10: He answered thus: ‘It is more than this — may a thousand praises be upon Giv.’
sentences.s333-s11: May the Lord of the world be his ally, and may the hearts of his ill‑wishers be full of thorns.
sentences.s333-s12: More or less, our lot is purely in your hand; may you be bright‑souled and your body sound.
